label: ai
topic: A Chinese-American adoptee's relationship with dumplings as identity
text:
The first dumplings you remember come from a frosted plastic bag bought at the SuperTarget in Edina, Minnesota. You are six years old, sitting at the kitchen island. Your white mother serves them on a paper towel to soak up the grease, alongside a dipping sauce made of Kikkoman soy sauce and Heinz white vinegar. They are microwaveable potstickers, their edges rubbery, the pork filling a dense, salty mystery. You eat them with a fork, swinging your legs, perfectly happy. You do not yet know you are a translation missing half its words.

In fourth grade, you bring leftovers in a plastic thermos. When you unscrew the lid, the smell of garlic and steamed cabbage blooms in the cafeteria air. A boy named Tyler pinches his nose. "What is that?" he asks, his face twisting. "Potstickers," you say, your voice small. He says it smells like hot garbage. You slide the thermos back into your lunchbox, your face burning. For the rest of the year, you beg for Lunchables. You learn early that assimilation is about eliminating odors, about taking up as little atmospheric space as possible.

You are twelve when your parents take you to San Francisco's Chinatown for a "heritage tour." They mean well. They steer you into a cavernous dim sum palace where chandeliers drip with fake crystal and women push steaming metal carts. Har gow, siu mai, char siu bao. The waitress stops at your table and speaks to you in rapid, musical Cantonese. You stare at her blankly, your cheeks flushing. "She doesn't speak it," your father explains apologetically. The waitress's eyes slide over you, recalibrating, dismissing. You bite into a shrimp dumpling. The wrapper is translucent, sticky, perfect. You chew, feeling like a ghost haunting a feast meant for the living. You look Chinese, but you are a forgery.

At your high school graduation dinner, the family gathers at a local Pan-Asian bistro. You order crab rangoon. It arrives deep-fried, shaped like a star, oozing with cream cheese. It is a wildly American invention, a bastardization of a culture. You look at the crispy shell, dipping it into neon-red sweet and sour sauce. It is delicious, and you hate yourself for loving it. You are a hybrid, just like the rangoon, stripped of authenticity, built for Midwestern palates.

Everything fractures during your junior year of college. You study abroad not in China, but in Kyoto, Japan. You tell yourself it's because of the language requirement, but really, you are avoiding the motherland that might reject you outright. One rainy Tuesday, you duck into a tiny, smoke-filled gyoza bar. The chef is an older man with a towel wrapped around his head. The gyoza arrive blistering hot, delicate on one side, perfectly charred on the bottom. Served with chili oil and black vinegar.

The chef speaks to you in Japanese. You answer in halting, broken Japanese. He laughs, wiping down the counter. "American-chan," he calls you.

And there it is. Thousands of miles from the country you were born in, and the country you were raised in, eating a Chinese dish adapted by the Japanese, you finally weep. You bite into the gyoza, letting the scalding broth burn the roof of your mouth. The pain is sharp and grounding. The dumpling is just a pocket of meat and vegetables, sealed shut, trying to hold its shape under intense heat. You chew, wiping your eyes with the back of your hand, finally understanding that a translation doesn't have to be perfect to sustain you.
